payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
Most discussions of music provenance assume the artist signing a recording is somebody the system already knows about. They're on a label. They're registered with a performance rights organization. They have a distributor account. The institutions that already verify their identity provide the trust anchor that makes the cryptographic signature meaningful.

That covers a meaningful slice of working musicians. It doesn't cover the long tail.

The institutional shortcut

A signature only proves what the system already trusts. If a recording is signed by Maria Sanchez, and the verification system knows that Maria Sanchez has been registered with a rights organization for six years, has released three albums through Distribution Company X, and has a verified Recording Academy membership, the signature has real weight. It's not just a string of numbers. It's a string of numbers attached to an identity that several independent institutions have already vetted.

When the institutions are already in place, the cryptographic layer is the easy part.

What happens when the institutions aren't there

Consider the producer who's been releasing tracks under a pseudonym from a city without a music industry presence. They have no rights organization membership. They have no label. They self-distribute through a service that doesn't perform identity verification. They're a real human making real music. They have nothing the verification system can hang an identity claim on.

Under the current administrative system, this isn't a problem because the current system doesn't really verify identity either. Anyone with an email address can upload a track. The verification gap exists, but it's hidden because the system isn't trying to do verification in the first place.

A provenance framework changes that. If the framework promises verifiable authorship, it has to deliver verifiable authorship for everyone, not just artists who happen to be embedded in the institutions. Otherwise the framework re-creates the same gatekeeping it claimed to dissolve.

What a solution looks like, and what it requires

Who vouches for the independent artist?

Most discussions of music provenance assume the artist signing a recording is somebody the system already knows. There's a label, a performance rights organization that registered them years ago, a distributor account under their legal name. Those institutions already verify identity, and that verification is the trust anchor that makes a cryptographic signature mean anything.

That covers a meaningful slice of working musicians. It doesn't cover the long tail.

The institutional shortcut

A signature only proves what the system already trusts. Say a recording is signed by Maria Sanchez. The verification system knows that name: six years with a rights organization, three albums through a real distributor, a Recording Academy membership on file. The signature carries weight. It isn't just a string of numbers. It's a string of numbers attached to an identity several independent institutions have already vetted.

When the institutions are in place, the cryptographic layer is the easy part.

What happens when the institutions aren't there

Consider the producer releasing tracks under a pseudonym from a city with no music industry to speak of. No label, no rights organization, a self-distribution service that never asks who they are. A real person making real music, and nothing for the verification system to hang an identity claim on.

Right now this isn't a problem, because the current system doesn't really verify identity either. Anyone with an email address can upload a track. The gap is there. It's just hidden, because nobody's claiming to close it.

A provenance framework changes that. If it promises verifiable authorship, it has to deliver verifiable authorship for everyone, not only the artists already embedded in the institutions. Otherwise it recreates the exact gatekeeping it claimed to dissolve.

What a solution looks like, and what it requires

Tiered approaches work in theory. The foundation is a stable signing key the producer keeps across releases, the same identity over time even when no central authority has formally verified who's behind it. From there it climbs.

Three tiers of identity verification
A stacked diagram showing three tiers of identity verification for independent artists. The wide base tier is a stable signing key over time. The narrower middle tier adds professional association membership. The narrowest top tier adds government identity. Each tier builds on the one below.

Base tier
Stable signing key over time
Reputation accumulates around the key

Middle tier
Adds professional association
membership

Top tier
Adds government identity
(optional)

The base tier is the one that matters for the long tail, because it asks nothing of an institution that doesn't exist yet. A producer who has signed forty releases over five years has built something real. No central authority vouches for the legal name, and the work still adds up to a track record anyone can verify against a single key.

That's the theory. In practice, the base tier needs coordination among professional associations, distributors, and the developers shipping the recording software that issues the keys to begin with. None of that is built. Until it is, the framework serves the artists who were already easy to verify and comes up short for the rest, and the rest are the same people the current system serves worst.

This is the part I don't have a finished answer for. The cryptographic primitives are decades old. The infrastructure they lean on, for artists without institutional ties, hasn't been built. That's the work the next decade has to do, and the people who do it well will determine whether provenance becomes a real democratization of authorship credit or another structure that rewards the artists who were already winning.
payload.title: Who vouches for the independent artist?
